task:
<command-message>superpowers:brainstorming</command-message>
<command-name>/superpowers:brainstorming</command-name>
<command-args>i would like to be able to unit test workflow generation - start with a plan, then run an agent that build the workflow.py file, and inspect the results. i would like to test a couple cases, from simple bug fixes to feature dev, in different contexts: on test repo with no instructions regarding the dev livecycle, and test repos where we have more instructions regarding dev, like run playwright (if frontent), always update docs, etc</command-args>

Yes, this is right. I want to test what kind of workflows agent creates given the guidelines and I would then potentially improve my prompt, you know, do evals and improve the skill, basically the workflow skill.
user_feedback_text: Yes, this is right. I want to test what kind of workflows agent creates given the guidelines and I would then potentially improve my prompt, you know, do evals and improve the skill, basically the workflow skill.

task: I mean, can't we just look at the model for choices and things like that if we import and inspect the state graph object?

task: 1. I really want to run cloud because the goal of those tests is to evaluate the workflow skill and make sure it actually is generating useful/efficient workflows.
user_feedback_text: 1. I really want to run cloud because the goal of those tests is to evaluate the workflow skill and make sure it actually is generating useful/efficient workflows.

task: That's good. Now I'm wondering about how do we evaluate the output, like the actual workflow that was built. Should we use another LLM to do that or is there a way we could use just regular Python assertions? I'd rather use Python assertions because this is faster, deterministic and of course free.
Yes, why not? We could add more scenarios.
yes
[Request interrupted by user]
continue
[Request interrupted by user]
Can you compare both approaches? Honestly, initially I was thinking of option B, but I know like parameterized fixtures are also very efficient.
user_feedback_text: Can you compare both approaches? Honestly, initially I was thinking of option B, but I know like parameterized fixtures are also very efficient.

task: B

Okay, yes, for point one, yes, makes sense. And also, yeah, that as a requirement in the workflow skill itself. That said, I think we already could run the script with dash dash diagram without execution. I did that multiple times and it was instantaneous.

And for point two, yeah, you can also do that small engine change.
yes
looks right

can we install/give the workflow skill to be closer to how things run reallY?

also, what does 'These are manual evals, not CI. Run them when iterating on the `/workflow` skill prompt.' mean? 

i want the evals to be pytests
